California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 21,
1993
Richard D. Jones
Olen Pointe Building
Three Pointe Drive,
Suite 120
Brea,
CA
92621
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—93—389
Dear Mr.
Jones:
This is in response to your request for a confirmation of
telephone advice provided to your office regarding the current
state of the contribution limits of the Political Reform Act (the
“Act”)
1 in light of the decisions in Service Employees
International Union,
AFL-CIO, et al. v.
Fair Political Practices
Commission (9th Cir.
1992)
955 F.2d 1312, cert. denied,
U.S.
,
112
S. Ct.
3056
(“SEIU”),
and Taxpayers to Limit Campaign
Spending v. Fair Political Practices Commission
(1990)
51 Cal.3d
744
(“Taxpayers”).
Please be aware that the conclusion in your October 11,
1993,
letter does not accurately reflect the current state of the law.
As I discussed with Bob Allen of your office,
a United States
District Court issued an order in the SEIU case which invalidated
the fiscal year contribution limitations of the Act.
2
(Seàtions
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Please note that the court did not invalidate the special
election and special runoff election contribution limits of
Proposition 73.
Therefore, these restrictions remain operative in
both local and statewide special elections.
(Section 85305.)
Moreover,
Section 85304 continues to prohibit candidates for state
or local office from transferring campaign funds to any candidate
who is subject to any valid contribution limits,
including those
limits established by local jurisdictions.
(See,
Weldy Advice
Letter,
No.
1-92-124.)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1-93-389
Page
2
85301,
85302,
and 85303; Lowell Advice Letter,
1—91—064.)
Thus,
the provisions of Proposition 73 providing fiscal year limits are
no longer being enforced in any context.
Moreover, recall elections are considered to be measures
rather than candidate elections and historically, have not been
subject to contribution limits.
(See, Roberti Advice Letter, A-
89—358.)
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-5901.
Sincerely,
I
Johi W. Wallace
Cou!sel,
Legal Division
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

sections.conclusion: The conclusion in your October 11, 1993, letter does not accurately reflect the current state of the law. A United States District Court issued an order in the SEIU case which invalidated the fiscal year contribution limitations of the Act (Sections 85301, 85302, and 85303). The special election and special runoff election contribution limits of Proposition 73 remain operative. Section 85304 continues to prohibit candidates from transferring campaign funds to candidates subject to valid contribution limits. Recall elections are not subject to contribution limits.
sections.question_synthetic: What is the current state of contribution limits under the Political Reform Act following the SEIU and Taxpayers court decisions, and does the requester's October 11, 1993 letter accurately reflect these limits?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that fiscal year contribution limits under the Political Reform Act have been invalidated by court order, but special election limits and prohibitions on campaign fund transfers remain in effect. Recall elections continue to be exempt from contribution limits.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION section header present; question synthesized from context of letter. CONCLUSION synthesized from body text. Document is informal advice letter responding to prior telephone advice and written inquiry. References multiple court cases and statutory sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What is the current state of contribution limits under the Political Reform Act following the SEIU and Taxpayers court decisions, and does the requester's October 11, 1993 letter accurately reflect these limits?

CONCLUSION: The conclusion in your October 11, 1993, letter does not accurately reflect the current state of the law. A United States District Court issued an order in the SEIU case which invalidated the fiscal year contribution limitations of the Act (Sections 85301, 85302, and 85303). The special election and special runoff election contribution limits of Proposition 73 remain operative. Section 85304 continues to prohibit candidates from transferring campaign funds to candidates subject to valid contribution limits. Recall elections are not subject to contribution limits.

Fair Political Practices Commission (9th Cir. 1992) 955 F.2d 1312, cert. denied, U.S. , 112 S. Ct. 3056 (“SEIU”), and Taxpayers to Limit Campaign Spending v. Fair Political Practices Commission (1990) 51 Cal.3d 744 (“Taxpayers”). Please be aware that the conclusion in your October 11, 1993, letter does not accurately reflect the current state of the law. As I discussed with Bob Allen of your office, a United States District Court issued an order in the SEIU case which invalidated the fiscal year contribution limitations of the Act. 2 (Seàtions 1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections 18000—18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 2 Please note that the court did not invalidate the special election and special runoff election contribution limits of Proposition 73. Therefore, these restrictions remain operative in both local and statewide special elections. (Section 85305.) Moreover, Section 85304 continues to prohibit candidates for state or local office from transferring campaign funds to any candidate who is subject to any valid contribution limits, including those limits established by local jurisdictions. (See, Weldy Advice Letter, No. 1-92-124.) 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Thus, the provisions of Proposition 73 providing fiscal year limits are no longer being enforced in any context. Moreover, recall elections are considered to be measures rather than candidate elections and historically, have not been subject to contribution limits. (See, Roberti Advice Letter, A- 89—358.) embedding.summary: FPPC response to Richard D. Jones confirming that a federal court decision invalidated fiscal year contribution limits under the Political Reform Act, while special election limits and related prohibitions remain operative.
